definition: A class that exists mainly to forward calls to another class, adding little or no additional behavior or abstraction. It increases indirection, surface area, and maintenance burden without providing clear benefits.
ruleOfThumb: If a type's public API is mostly one-to-one delegations, inline it or merge responsibilities unless the intermediary adds validation, orchestration, caching, or a meaningful abstraction.

citation.author: Martin Fowler
citation.bookTitle: Refactoring: Improving the Design of Existing Code

questions.prompt: Which statement best describes this maintainability problem?

options.text: A type that mostly forwards calls to another type and doesn't contribute meaningful behavior.
options.explanation: Correct — the class's methods are thin pass-throughs. It adds indirection and more surface area to maintain without encapsulating new logic such as validation, transaction orchestration, or anti-corruption.

options.text: A helper utility offering only static functions used across unrelated modules.
options.explanation: Incorrect — a pure static utility is about global shared behavior, not an instance wrapper that delegates to another instance. The costs and refactor are different (extracting functions vs removing an extra layer).

options.text: A god object accumulating many unrelated responsibilities.
options.explanation: Incorrect — a god object centralizes responsibilities; here the shown service does not consolidate multiple domains, it merely forwards to a repository.

options.text: A facade that intentionally simplifies a complex subsystem by exposing a smaller API surface.
options.explanation: Incorrect — a proper facade intentionally reduces complexity and hides details. In this snippet the service exposes nearly identical methods to the underlying type, so it doesn't simplify or aggregate behavior.

questions.prompt: Look at the service and repository. What's the most actionable refactor to improve clarity?

options.text: Remove or inline the thin service and let callers use the repository directly, unless the service adds real behavior.
options.explanation: Correct — the service only forwards calls. Inlining or collapsing the service reduces indirection and test/mocking surface. Keep a separate layer only when it performs orchestration, validation, caching, or a domain abstraction.

options.text: Split the repository into multiple finer-grained repositories to satisfy single responsibility.
options.explanation: Plausible but off-target — splitting repositories addresses overlarge data concerns; it doesn't fix a delegating layer that adds no behavior. If the repo is already cohesive, splitting doesn't remove the extra wrapper.

options.text: Introduce an interface for the repository to decouple concrete implementation from callers.
options.explanation: Also reasonable, yet orthogonal — introducing an interface helps substitution and testing but keeps the extra delegation layer intact. The primary issue here is redundant forwarding, which remains after introducing an interface.

options.text: Wrap repository calls in try/catch to avoid leaking persistence errors to higher layers.
options.explanation: Handling errors is important, but adding try/catch in a forwarding layer only addresses error handling, not the unnecessary indirection. If error translation is the goal, then the service should implement that behavior explicitly.

questions.prompt: Two options to address a thin delegating layer. Which is the better long-term design and why?

options.text: Prefer Version A: keep the thin delegating service as a stable indirection layer across the codebase.
options.explanation: Not ideal — preserving a forwarding-only layer everywhere increases API surface, multiplies trivial tests and mocks, and hides the fact that there is no abstraction. Over time it becomes dead weight; it also makes cross-cutting refactors harder because you must update many trivial delegations.

options.text: Prefer Version B: remove the wrapper where it adds no value; either use the underlying component directly or merge responsibilities into a domain-focused service when behavior is needed.
options.explanation: Better long-term — eliminating unnecessary indirection reduces code to what actually matters. When callers use the underlying component directly, intent is clearer and fewer types are maintained. If new behavior arises, consolidate repo + trivial layer into a meaningful domain service that encapsulates rules, orchestration, or side effects. This follows practical refactoring guidance to keep abstractions meaningful (see Fowler) and reduces test/mocking complexity (see Clean Code).
